topic: Someone finds out their medical records were mixed up with another patient's
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout landed on my desk with a soft, sickening thud. My hands, slick with a cold sweat, trembled so violently I had to grip the edge of the mahogany surface to steady myself. It was a medical summary, detailed and chillingly familiar, but the name on the header was wrong. It belonged to Mr. Henderson. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that did nothing to calm the frantic drumming beneath my ribs. I reread the diagnostic codes, the medication lists, the frightening prognosis, and a high-pitched, almost inaudible sound escaped my throat. I pushed the papers away, the movement jerky, and began pacing the length of the room, my eyes darting around as if expecting someone to materialize and correct this grotesque error. Every muscle in my body felt coiled tight, vibrating with an unbearable tension. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. "This isn't right. This is impossible."

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the fluorescent office light buzzing overhead seeming to magnify the sheer absurdity of the situation. The names didn't match. My own file, the one I’d been meticulously reviewing, was inexplicably interwoven with someone else’s history—a stranger’s life, their vulnerabilities, their worst fears now laid bare in stark, clinical text. A wave of nausea washed over me, so profound it made my vision swim at the edges. I slapped my forehead, a dull, heavy sound in the quiet room, and stood up abruptly. My legs felt unsteady, like they were submerged in water. I began to pace in tight circles, my gaze fixed on the wall, unable to settle on any single point. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my temples. "Someone messed this up," I managed, my voice tight and strained, laced with a frantic edge that I couldn't seem to control.

[story 3]

When the file opened, the reality fractured. It wasn't just a clerical mistake; it was an invasion. Every entry, every chart detail, felt like a violation. I sat down heavily in my chair, the springs groaning under the sudden weight of my distress. My hands began to shake uncontrollably, a rapid, fine tremor that made holding a pen impossible. I tried to focus on the text, but my eyes kept jumping from line to line, unable to process the sheer wrongness of seeing another person’s intimate health data so carelessly displayed in my own space. A hot flush spread across my neck and face. I started tapping my fingers rapidly on the desk, a frantic, uneven rhythm that escalated quickly into a desperate hammering. "This can't be real," I mumbled, leaning forward, my whole body rigid with a desperate need to either grab the papers and destroy them or scream until the walls shook.
